skill.name: react-19-patterns
skill.description: React 19 guidance for building Server Components, Client Components, hooks, Suspense, streaming, transitions, and migration patterns with TypeScript.
skill.summary: Apply React 19 component, hook, Suspense, streaming, and migration patterns correctly.
skill.author: Barnhardt-Enterprises-Inc

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 739 potential issues, but review found the high-volume command, script, network, environment, and storage hits are primarily Markdown examples or validator rule data. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, obfuscation, or malicious runtime behavior was confirmed. The skill is low risk, with a note that documentation examples using secrets and external APIs require careful adaptation.

medium_findings.title: Secret-Bearing Network Examples Require Review
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes examples that pass environment variable values in headers for external API requests. This is a legitimate server-side pattern, but copied code must be reviewed so real secrets are only sent to intended services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is real in documentation examples and includes secret-like environment variable names. I did not find evidence of malicious exfiltration because the snippets are educational and not executed by the skill.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command detections come from Markdown inline code, TypeScript snippets, or documented validator usage. The reviewed lines do not invoke Ruby backticks or shell execution inside executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples are Markdown documentation or React import syntax. The executable validator does not use subprocess, shell, eval, or dynamic command construction.

low_findings.title: Browser Storage References Are Instructional Examples
low_findings.description: Browser storage hits appear in examples that explain client-only storage usage or validator rule definitions. They do not collect marketplace user data or transmit browser storage values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The storage references are either marked as React examples or listed as strings for validation checks. No evidence was found that the skill reads actual browser storage at runtime.

low_findings.title: Local Validator Performs Intended Filesystem Reads
low_findings.description: The validator accepts a user-provided file or directory and reads React files to report pattern issues. This is intended functionality and no network transmission or secret harvesting was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem access is confirmed in executable Python code, but its purpose is local validation. Confidence is high for the access and moderate for low impact because users choose the path.

low_findings.title: Aggregate Dangerous-Combination Heuristic Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The critical static heuristic combined separate documentation examples for code loading, network calls, filesystem access, and credential names. Review found no single malicious flow that gathers credentials and sends them externally.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The heuristic is based on real tokens spread across documentation and validator code. Semantic review did not find a connected execution path or prompt instruction that performs exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation Shows Secrets in External Request Headers
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples demonstrate environment variables used in Authorization or API key headers for external requests. This pattern is common, but it should be adapted carefully to avoid leaking secrets to unintended endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code examples are explicit and easy to copy into an app. The risk depends on the final endpoint and secret ownership, so this is a pattern warning rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Local File Scanning
dangerous_patterns.description: The validator recursively finds .tsx and .jsx files under a supplied directory. This is expected for a code validator, but users should avoid running it on directories that contain unrelated sensitive files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The recursive scan is directly present in the validator. Impact is limited because it filters React file extensions and does not transmit file contents.

content.value_statement: React 19 changes how teams use components, hooks, Server Actions, Suspense, and streaming. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for correct TypeScript implementations.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Server Components versus Client Components.
Provides React 19 hook patterns, including use, useOptimistic, useFormStatus, and useActionState.
Guides Suspense boundaries, streaming layouts, transitions, and optimistic user interfaces.
Includes migration guidance for React 18 projects moving to React 19.
Includes a Python validator for common React component and hook mistakes.
limitations: The validator uses pattern matching and can miss complex React code paths.
Examples are educational and must be adapted to each application architecture.
It does not replace official React documentation or framework-specific release notes.
It does not validate security, accessibility, or performance for a full application.

use_cases.title: Plan a New React 19 Feature
use_cases.description: Choose the right component type, hook pattern, Suspense boundary, and data-loading approach before implementation.

use_cases.title: Review Component Architecture
use_cases.description: Check whether a React codebase separates server data work from client interactivity and state management.

use_cases.title: Migrate From React 18
use_cases.description: Identify legacy APIs, update rendering patterns, and introduce React 19 features in controlled steps.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Component Type
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the React 19 patterns skill, decide whether this component should be a Server Component or Client Component. Explain the reason and list required changes.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Hook Usage
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React component for Rules of Hooks issues, missing dependency arrays, and invalid React 19 patterns. Give a concise fix plan.

prompt_templates.title: Design Suspense Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a React 19 Suspense and streaming structure for this page. Include boundary placement, fallback behavior, and data-loading order.

prompt_templates.title: Migrate Complex App
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a React 18 to React 19 migration plan for this application. Cover removed APIs, Server Components, Server Actions, transitions, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: A product page fetches data, filters results, and opens a cart drawer.
output_examples.output: The response separates product data into a Server Component and moves filter state and the cart drawer into small Client Components.

output_examples.input: A form needs optimistic updates and server-side persistence.
output_examples.output: The response recommends useOptimistic for immediate feedback, useActionState for server result state, and useFormStatus for pending submit controls.

output_examples.input: A React 18 app still uses legacy rendering and string refs.
output_examples.output: The response lists migration steps for root rendering, ref replacement, hook checks, and validation with the included React pattern validator.

best_practices: Default to Server Components unless the component needs browser APIs, state, effects, or event handlers.
Keep Client Components small and pass serializable props from server-rendered parents.
Place Suspense boundaries around meaningful loading units and avoid unnecessary nested boundaries.
anti_patterns: Do not make Client Components async for data fetching.
Do not call hooks inside conditions, loops, or regular helper functions.
Do not copy example secret names or API calls without reviewing your security model.

faq.question: Does this skill write React code for me?
faq.answer: It provides patterns, review guidance, and examples that an AI assistant can apply to your React code.

faq.question: Does it support TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes. The guides and examples focus on React 19 with TypeScript patterns.

faq.question: Can it validate an existing project?
faq.answer: It includes a Python validator that checks common React file patterns in files or directories.

faq.question: Is it only for Next.js?
faq.answer: No. Many patterns apply broadly, but Server Components and Server Actions depend on framework support.

faq.question: Does it cover React 19 hooks?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers standard hooks and React 19 hooks such as use, useOptimistic, useFormStatus, and useActionState.

faq.question: Can it replace a full code review?
faq.answer: No. It helps identify React pattern issues, but humans should still review security, accessibility, and product behavior.
